text: In the early morning hours of April 18, 1906, a massive earthquake along the San Andreas Fault struck San Francisco, triggering fires that raged for days and ultimately caused far more destruction than the initial shaking. The disaster killed more than 3,000 people, destroyed over 28,000 buildings with flames sweeping across hundreds of city blocks and leveling much of the city. In the aftermath of the earthquake, an estimated 75,000 citizens simply left San Francisco. The remaining homeless population of 250,000 established makeshift camps. #HISTORY

No leads, no suspects… until a dog cracks the case 🐕 The stolen World Cup trophy mystery takes an unbelievable turn thanks to an unexpected hero. Watch new episodes of The UnBelievable with Dan Aykroyd Fridays at 9/8c on HISTORY! #TheUnBelievable

As spring begins, cherry blossom trees, known as sakura, burst into bloom across Japan. They blanket mountainsides, parks and gardens in shades of pink and white—a phenomenon that attracts an estimated 63 million people each year to behold the nation’s beloved blossoms. From ancient times, farmers viewed the blooms as a divine signal to start planting rice seedlings. And the earliest hanami, which literally translates to “flower viewing,” were held in the hopes of a bountiful harvest. To learn more about this tradition, visit the link in our bio. #HISTORY

On April 14th, 1912, the Titanic struck an iceberg. 

#HISTORY

A trade war. A global superpower. A president betting on the power of the U.S. economy.

In 1807, Thomas Jefferson tries it first—cutting off American trade to force Britain to back down.

But when the economy collapses and unrest spreads, the strategy starts to look less like a solution… and more like a crisis.

What can Jefferson’s trade war teach us today?

Listen to the newest episode of HISTORY This Week.

A diagnosis made while sleeping… and a cure no one expected 😳 When a baby’s condition turns critical, an unlikely solution brings a shocking and immediate turnaround. Watch new episodes Fridays at 9/8c on HISTORY! #TheUnBelievable

On April 11, 1945, just months before World War II ended, U.S. soldiers from 6th Armored Division of the Third Army liberated the Buchenwald concentration camp—one of the Nazi regime’s largest and most brutal camps, where prisoners endured forced labor, starvation, disease, executions, and inhumane medical experiments. American forces found thousands of emaciated prisoners still inside, including future Nobel laureate Elie Wiesel. To learn more about what happened on #ThisDayInHistory, visit the link in our bio. #HISTORY
